content.problemStatement:
Chef has an integer sequence **a1**, **a2**, ..., **aN** of size **N**, where all the elements of the sequence are **0** initially. Chef also has **M** segments, here the **i**th one is **[Li, Ri]**. He wants to create new sequences using the following operation:


In a single operation, he picks a segment from the **M** segments. Let the chosen segment be **[s, t]**.
Then flip the all elements from **s**th to **t**th elements, namely, **ai** is changed to **1 − ai** for all **s ≤ i ≤ t**.
Chef can perform the operation as many times as Chef likes. Chef wants to know how many different sequences Chef can make. Since the answer can be very large, please print it modulo **1000000007 (109+7)**.
content.inputFormat: First line contains two space-separated integers N and M, denoting the size of the sequence and the number of segments respectively. Then the i-th line of the next M lines contains two space-separated integers Li and Ri, denoting the i-th segments.
content.outputFormat: Print a single line containing one integer, denoting the number of sequences which can be created by Chef, modulo 1000000007 (10^9+7).

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by iterating over each position in the sequence and checking how many segments cover that position. If a position is covered by at least one segment, it can be either 0 or 1, resulting in two possibilities. The total number of possible sequences is the product of the number of possibilities for each position.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * M) per test case, O(T * N * M) total.
